input.topic: Is GPT-4o-mini suitable for production use?

sources.title: OpenAI announcement (July 2024)
sources.content: GPT-4o-mini scores 82% on MMLU vs GPT-4o's 88%. Pricing is $0.15/$0.60 per million input/output tokens — about 6% of GPT-4o's cost. Recommended for production workloads where cost-efficiency matters more than the last few percent of quality. 128k context window, multimodal (text + vision).

sources.title: Independent benchmark — Vellum AI (Sept 2024)
sources.content: On reasoning benchmarks (GSM8K, MATH, HumanEval), GPT-4o-mini scores slightly below Claude Haiku 3.5 ($0.80/$4.00 per M tokens) but ahead of Gemini Flash. On instruction-following and JSON-mode reliability, it leads its price tier. Latency p50 ~800ms for typical chat completions.

sources.title: Production case study — Notion AI
sources.content: Notion migrated 60% of its AI features from GPT-4 to GPT-4o-mini in Q3 2024. They report 4x throughput, 92% cost reduction, and <2% quality regression on their internal evals. Highlighted reliability concern: structured-output drift requires periodic prompt re-tuning.

sources.title: Reddit thread — r/MachineLearning
sources.content: Practitioners report GPT-4o-mini struggles with long-context retrieval beyond ~30k tokens despite the advertised 128k window. Several users observed quality cliff at 40k+ tokens for QA-over-documents tasks.

expected.executive_summary: GPT-4o-mini is broadly suitable for production at ~6% the cost of GPT-4o [1], with Notion's migration providing a strong reference point (4x throughput, 92% cost reduction, <2% quality regression) [3]. However, the model underperforms Claude Haiku 3.5 on reasoning benchmarks [2] and shows quality degradation on long-context retrieval beyond ~30k tokens despite the 128k advertised window [4]. Recommendation: suitable for cost-sensitive chat/summarization/classification workloads under 30k context; pair with Claude Haiku for reasoning-heavy or long-context tasks.

key_points.claim: GPT-4o-mini is ~6% of GPT-4o's cost, ~82% MMLU vs GPT-4o's 88%
Underperforms Claude Haiku 3.5 on reasoning benchmarks (GSM8K, MATH, HumanEval)
Leads its price tier on instruction-following and JSON-mode reliability
Notion migration: 4x throughput, 92% cost reduction, <2% quality regression
Quality cliff at 40k+ tokens for long-context QA workloads

disagreements: Source [1] frames GPT-4o-mini as broadly production-ready; source [2] calls out a reasoning gap vs Claude Haiku.
Source [1] advertises 128k context; source [4] reports practical quality degradation beyond ~30k.
open_questions: What specific reasoning workloads matter for our use case (cost-sensitive vs reasoning-heavy)?
Does our context length distribution typically stay under 30k? If so, the long-context concern doesn't apply.

input.topic: What are the current EU regulatory requirements for AI agents handling personal data?

sources.title: EU AI Act — Final text (March 2024)
sources.content: The EU AI Act classifies AI systems into 4 risk tiers. 'High-risk' systems (which include AI making decisions about employment, credit, public services, law enforcement) require: risk management system, data governance documentation, technical documentation, human oversight, accuracy/robustness/cybersecurity, and conformity assessment. Effective from August 2026 with phased compliance deadlines.

sources.title: GDPR Article 22 + EDPB guidance (2023)
sources.content: Article 22 of GDPR grants individuals the right not to be subject to solely automated decisions producing legal or significant effects. AI agents handling personal data must: (1) provide opt-out mechanism unless covered by exception; (2) implement human review for significant decisions; (3) inform data subjects about the logic involved. The 2023 EDPB guidance clarified that 'AI assistant' systems must disclose automated-decision involvement.

sources.title: ICO (UK) AI guidance update (Feb 2024)
sources.content: UK has diverged from EU AI Act but maintains GDPR-equivalent rules via UK GDPR. ICO's 2024 guidance: AI systems must conduct DPIA (Data Protection Impact Assessment), implement appropriate technical safeguards (encryption at rest + in transit, access logging, model audit trail), and provide explainability documentation suitable for the technical sophistication of the data subject.

sources.title: Legal commentary — White & Case (April 2024)
sources.content: The EU AI Act + GDPR create overlapping obligations for AI agents processing personal data. Companies face dual compliance: GDPR for the data processing layer, AI Act for the AI system itself. Critically: the AI Act's 'high-risk' classification triggers ADDITIONAL obligations beyond GDPR — fundamental rights impact assessment, registration in the EU database, post-market monitoring. Penalties up to €35M or 7% of global revenue.

expected.executive_summary: AI agents handling personal data in the EU face dual compliance: GDPR (data processing layer) + AI Act (the AI system itself) [4]. Key obligations: GDPR Article 22 — opt-out, human review for significant decisions, automated-decision disclosure [2]; EU AI Act — for high-risk systems, risk management, data governance docs, human oversight, conformity assessment, phased compliance from August 2026 [1]; ICO/UK — DPIA, technical safeguards, explainability docs [3]. High-risk classification (employment, credit, public services, law enforcement) triggers significant ADDITIONAL obligations including EU database registration and post-market monitoring [4]. Penalties up to €35M or 7% of global revenue [4]. Recommendation: classify the agent's risk tier first, then map obligations.

key_points.claim: EU AI Act classifies AI into 4 risk tiers; high-risk systems have extensive obligations effective August 2026
GDPR Article 22 grants right against solely automated decisions; requires opt-out + human review + disclosure
UK ICO requires DPIA, technical safeguards, and explainability documentation
Dual compliance — GDPR for data + AI Act for the AI system
High-risk classification triggers additional obligations: fundamental rights impact assessment, EU database registration, post-market monitoring
Penalties up to €35M or 7% of global revenue

disagreements: No direct disagreement — sources are complementary across the regulatory layers.
open_questions: What risk tier does our agent fall into? (drives most obligations)
Are we processing personal data of EU subjects, or just data referencing them? (changes GDPR applicability)
Do we need a DPO (Data Protection Officer) — depends on processing volume + tier

input.topic: Top competitors to MDK in the AI agent platform space

sources.title: Gartner Magic Quadrant — Generative AI Application Platforms (Q1 2025)
sources.content: Gartner names LangChain, LlamaIndex, Microsoft Azure AI Studio, Google Vertex AI Agent Builder, and Amazon Bedrock Agents as Leaders. Visionaries include CrewAI, AutoGen (Microsoft Research), and Haystack. Notable Niche Players: Movate, Inworld, Fixie. The report emphasizes that evaluation tooling is a top-3 differentiator in 2025.

sources.title: Open-source landscape analysis — DevTools Insights (March 2025)
sources.content: LangChain dominates GitHub stars (90k+) and weekly downloads, but adoption sentiment has shifted in 2024-2025: practitioners cite breaking changes, abstraction churn, and production-readiness gaps. LlamaIndex (35k stars) is gaining as the RAG-focused alternative. New entrants — Mastra, Vercel AI SDK, and Movate — are targeting the 'production-first' niche with stronger eval tooling and explicit observability.

sources.title: Practitioner survey — AI Engineer Conference 2025
sources.content: Survey of 1200 AI engineers building production agents. Top tools by current use: 1) LangChain (42%), 2) custom-built (28%), 3) Vercel AI SDK (12%), 4) LlamaIndex (9%), 5) Azure AI Studio (5%). Top tools by 12-month adoption intent: 1) custom-built (35%), 2) Vercel AI SDK (24%), 3) LangChain (18%, declining), 4) LlamaIndex (12%), 5) Mastra/Movate-tier production-first platforms (8%).

sources.title: Movate competitive analysis — internal (March 2025)
sources.content: Movate's primary differentiation: declarative agent.yaml + 10-category scorecard + Azure-first deploy. Direct overlap: LangChain (largest, but framework rather than platform), Vercel AI SDK (TypeScript-only, narrower deploy story), Mastra (closest competitor — also Azure-friendly + declarative). Indirect overlap: cloud-vendor platforms (Azure AI Studio, Vertex AI, Bedrock) — operators already on a cloud often prefer that vendor's solution despite weaker eval tooling.

expected.executive_summary: MDK competes in the 'production-first' AI agent platform tier alongside Mastra and Vercel AI SDK [2,3]. Direct competitors: Mastra (closest — declarative + Azure-friendly), Vercel AI SDK (TypeScript-only), LangChain (framework, not platform, but largest installed base — 42% practitioner share but declining sentiment) [1,3,4]. Indirect competition from cloud-vendor platforms (Azure AI Studio, Vertex AI Agent Builder, Bedrock Agents) — operators on a cloud often prefer vendor-native solutions [1,4]. Gartner positions MDK as Niche Player with emphasis that 'evaluation tooling is a top-3 differentiator in 2025' [1] — aligns with MDK's 10-category scorecard story. 'Custom-built' is 28% of current use and rising to 35% intent [3], indicating real market preference for code-owned solutions over heavyweight frameworks. Recommendation: lean into the production-first + eval-tooling story; the framework-vs-platform divide is a real wedge.

key_points.claim: Gartner Leaders: LangChain, LlamaIndex, Azure AI Studio, Vertex AI Agent Builder, Bedrock Agents
Gartner Niche Players include Movate
Evaluation tooling is a top-3 differentiator per Gartner 2025
LangChain leads adoption at 42% but sentiment is shifting; LlamaIndex gaining as RAG-focused alternative
Production-first new entrants: Mastra, Vercel AI SDK, Movate
Mastra is MDK's closest direct competitor (declarative + Azure-friendly)
Custom-built tooling is 28% current use, rising to 35% adoption intent — competitor for everyone

disagreements: Gartner places cloud-vendor platforms as Leaders [1]; practitioner survey [3] gives them only 5% current use — suggests Gartner reflects enterprise sales motion while survey reflects developer preference.
open_questions: What is Mastra's pricing model + tier structure? (closest direct competitor)
How does our eval tooling actually compare on benchmarks vs Vercel AI SDK + custom-built solutions?
Should we have a 'custom-built / migrate from custom' story given that's the largest growing segment?

input.topic: Should we adopt Bun for our Node.js services?

sources.title: Bun 1.0 announcement (September 2023)
sources.content: Bun 1.0 announces drop-in Node.js compatibility, claims 3x faster cold start, native TypeScript execution, built-in package manager + test runner + bundler. Targets the 'JavaScript runtime' tier alongside Node.js and Deno.

sources.title: Production case study — Browserless (April 2024)
sources.content: Browserless migrated their core service from Node.js 20 to Bun in Q1 2024. Reports: 1.8x throughput improvement, 40% lower memory usage. Honest about pain: ecosystem gaps required ~2 weeks of workarounds for 4 npm packages that didn't run cleanly under Bun. They recommend it for greenfield services + caution for legacy stacks.

sources.title: Practitioner survey — State of JS 2024
sources.content: Bun retention rating: 4.2/5 (would use again) — highest among new runtimes. But only 18% of respondents have used it in production; 67% remain on Node.js. Top concern cited: ecosystem maturity, specifically around production-grade observability tools (Datadog, New Relic agents had partial support as of mid-2024).

sources.title: Vendor compatibility tracker — Vercel + Bun docs
sources.content: Vercel added official Bun support as a runtime option in Q4 2023. Notable gaps as of April 2024: Vercel Edge functions still require Node.js (Bun support TBD), some Vercel KV/Blob client SDKs untested under Bun. Other deploy platforms (Railway, Fly.io, Render) have similar 'supported but with caveats' positioning.

expected.executive_summary: Bun is production-viable for new services with clear performance wins (Browserless reports 1.8x throughput, 40% lower memory [2]) and high practitioner retention (4.2/5 [3]). However, only 18% adoption [3] and named ecosystem gaps — observability tool maturity [3], partial Vercel/cloud-platform support [4], occasional npm package incompatibility (~2-week mitigation per Browserless [2]) — make wholesale Node.js migration risky. Recommendation: adopt for greenfield services where the perf gains matter and the ecosystem gaps don't bite (e.g. internal tools, simple APIs); keep production legacy stacks on Node.js until observability + cloud-platform support reach parity (likely 2025-2026).

key_points.claim: Bun 1.0 claims 3x faster cold start, native TypeScript, drop-in Node.js compat
Browserless reports 1.8x throughput + 40% lower memory after Bun migration
Browserless found 2-week mitigation effort for 4 incompatible npm packages
Bun retention rating 4.2/5 (highest among new runtimes); only 18% production adoption
Observability tools (Datadog, New Relic) had partial Bun support as of mid-2024
Vercel supports Bun but Edge functions + some platform SDKs untested

disagreements: Source [1] frames Bun as drop-in Node.js compatible; sources [2] and [3] document real ecosystem gaps that contradict that framing.
open_questions: What's our observability stack? If we're on Datadog and need agent support, that may be the blocker.
Are any of our services candidates for greenfield rewrites in the next 6 months? (best fit)
Do we deploy to Vercel Edge or any platform where Bun support is incomplete?

input.topic: Snowflake vs BigQuery vs Databricks for analytics warehouse

sources.title: Independent TCO comparison — Fundamentals.dev (March 2025)
sources.content: TCO analysis on a 50TB workload: Snowflake $32k/mo (compute + storage + serverless), BigQuery $24k/mo (on-demand + storage), Databricks $28k/mo (DBU compute + Delta storage). Pricing model differences matter more than headline rate — BigQuery's on-demand favors bursty workloads, Snowflake's reserved capacity favors steady-state, Databricks' DBU favors long-running ML.

sources.title: Practitioner case study — Datadog scale data platform (Q4 2024)
sources.content: Datadog runs a multi-warehouse architecture: Snowflake for BI/analytics (5PB), BigQuery for ad-hoc data science (where their team prefers SQL latency), Databricks for ML feature engineering. Cite that single-vendor lock-in failed for them — different workload types optimize against different warehouses. Total spend ~$2M/yr across all three.

sources.title: Vendor differentiation — Bessemer Venture Partners thesis (2024)
sources.content: Snowflake leads on: separation of compute/storage, multi-cloud (AWS+GCP+Azure), data sharing (Snowflake Marketplace). BigQuery leads on: integrated AI/ML (Vertex), GCP-native cost (storage cheaper than Snowflake), serverless (no instance management). Databricks leads on: Delta Lake unified storage, ML/AI native pipelines, lakehouse architecture for cost-sensitive PB-scale workloads.

sources.title: Reddit r/dataengineering thread (April 2025)
sources.content: Consensus from the thread: Snowflake = easiest team adoption + best BI tooling integration. BigQuery = best $/query at scale, especially for one-off analyses. Databricks = best for ML-heavy or unstructured data shops. Pain points cited: Snowflake credit consumption is hard to predict for new teams; BigQuery's quotas + slot system has a learning curve; Databricks UX is dense and overwhelming for non-data-engineers.

expected.executive_summary: All three are viable; the choice depends on workload mix [1,2,3]. Snowflake wins on team adoption + BI tooling [4] and multi-cloud flexibility [3] — TCO ~$32k/mo for 50TB workload [1]. BigQuery is cheapest at $24k/mo [1], strongest for ad-hoc + bursty + GCP-native shops [3], with best $/query at scale [4]. Databricks at $28k/mo [1] wins for ML-heavy lakehouse use cases [3]. Datadog's reference architecture uses ALL THREE because different workloads optimize differently [2] — single-vendor lock-in failed for them. Recommendation: assess workload distribution before choosing. For BI-dominant: Snowflake. For GCP-native or bursty/ad-hoc: BigQuery. For ML-heavy or unstructured data: Databricks. If workload mix is balanced + budget allows, the Datadog multi-warehouse pattern is worth considering despite operational overhead.

key_points.claim: TCO on 50TB workload: Snowflake $32k/mo, BigQuery $24k/mo, Databricks $28k/mo
Pricing model matters more than rate — BigQuery favors bursty, Snowflake reserved, Databricks long-running ML
Snowflake strengths: multi-cloud, data sharing, BI integration
BigQuery strengths: integrated AI/ML, serverless, best $/query at scale
Databricks strengths: Delta Lake unified storage, ML pipelines, lakehouse cost efficiency at PB scale
Multi-warehouse architecture (Datadog reference): different workloads optimize against different warehouses

disagreements: No direct disagreement — sources point in consistent directions but emphasize different workload profiles.
open_questions: What's our current workload mix (BI vs ad-hoc vs ML)?
Are we already on AWS/GCP/Azure? (cloud co-location matters for egress costs)
What's our team's existing tool familiarity?

input.topic: How big is the AI agent platform market in 2025?

sources.title: Grand View Research — AI Agent Market 2025-2030
sources.content: Global AI agent market valued at $5.4B in 2024, projected to reach $47.1B by 2030 (44.8% CAGR). Drivers: enterprise automation, customer-service replacement, vertical-specific agents (legal, healthcare, finance). Largest segment: customer-service AI agents ($1.8B of 2024 market).

sources.title: MarketsAndMarkets — AI Agents Report 2025
sources.content: Forecasts AI agent platform market specifically (excluding end-user applications) at $1.2B in 2024, reaching $11.9B by 2029 (58.1% CAGR). North America 47% share, Europe 23%, APAC 21%. Top vertical markets: financial services, healthcare, retail.

sources.title: Andreessen Horowitz market commentary (Q1 2025)
sources.content: Caveats on the headline forecasts: most market sizing reports lump together genuine AI agent platforms with 'AI features' embedded in existing SaaS, inflating the addressable market. True AI agent platform TAM (pure-play platforms only) likely closer to $400M-$600M in 2024. a16z's investment thesis: the market will consolidate to 3-5 platforms in the next 3 years; current 40+ vendor landscape is unsustainable.

sources.title: Practitioner usage data — AI Engineer Conference 2025
sources.content: Among 1200 surveyed AI engineers: 38% are building production AI agents (up from 12% in 2023). Average team uses 2.3 different tools to build production agents (LangChain + custom + something else). 71% report their employer has a 'GenAI strategy' but only 28% have a dedicated 'AI platform team' — most agent work happens in product engineering teams.

expected.executive_summary: Headline market sizing varies widely: $47B by 2030 (Grand View, broad AI agent market) [1], $11.9B by 2029 (M&M, platform-specific) [2], or $400M-$600M today (a16z, pure-play platforms only) [3]. The discrepancy reflects what counts as 'AI agent platform' — broad reports include features-in-SaaS, narrow ones include only standalone platforms [3]. Growth signals are real: 38% of surveyed AI engineers building production agents (up from 12% in 2023) [4], CAGR projections 45-58% [1,2]. Caveat: a16z predicts platform consolidation to 3-5 winners in 3 years [3]; current 40+ vendor landscape is unsustainable. Recommendation: use the $400M-$1.2B 2024 figure as a conservative TAM for pure-play platform planning; use the $11.9B 2029 figure as the optimistic 5-year ceiling. The consolidation thesis [3] suggests platform vendors should prioritize building wedge before broad market saturation triggers shakeout.

key_points.claim: Broad AI agent market $5.4B in 2024 -> $47.1B by 2030 (44.8% CAGR)
AI agent platform market $1.2B in 2024 -> $11.9B by 2029 (58.1% CAGR)
Pure-play platform TAM closer to $400M-$600M in 2024 once feature-in-SaaS is excluded
38% of AI engineers building production agents (up from 12% in 2023)
Average team uses 2.3 different tools
Consolidation expected: 3-5 winners in 3 years from 40+ current vendors

disagreements: Sources [1] and [2] use broad definitions that inflate TAM; source [3] explicitly calls this out and quotes a much smaller pure-play number — directly disagreeing on methodology.
open_questions: Where on the broad-vs-narrow spectrum does MDK actually compete? (platform vs feature)
If consolidation hits 3-5 winners, is MDK a winner or acquisition target?
What does the customer-service segment specifically value — different from our enterprise positioning?

input.topic: Technical due diligence — assessing a Series A AI startup's tech stack

sources.title: First Round Capital — Technical due diligence checklist (2024)
sources.content: Six areas to assess: (1) Architecture — monolith vs microservices, scaling story; (2) Engineering quality — test coverage, CI/CD, deployment cadence; (3) Team — senior:junior ratio, on-call rotation, hire-to-attrition ratio; (4) Security — secrets management, access controls, SOC 2 readiness; (5) Data — provenance, retention, customer ownership rights; (6) Tech debt — what's bridging vs core, refactor cadence.

sources.title: Sequoia engineering excellence post — 'Code is policy' (2023)
sources.content: Argues that DD should focus on engineering culture artifacts: PR templates, deploy frequency, MTTR (mean time to recovery), incident retros, README quality. These reveal more than architecture diagrams. Specific red flags: no incident retros, deploy frequency <weekly, MTTR >24h for P1s.

sources.title: Practitioner write-up — 'What I saw doing 50 tech DDs' (Hacker News 2024)
sources.content: Author conducted 50 Series A-stage technical due diligences over 3 years. Most common issues found: (1) test coverage 0-20% (40% of companies); (2) production secrets in environment-variable text files (30%); (3) no documented backup/restore procedure (50%); (4) single key engineer with no successor in critical area (60%). Conclusion: 'engineering excellence' is rare at Series A; the question is whether founders recognize the gaps and have a credible plan.

sources.title: Y Combinator — 'Engineering at YC scale' (2024)
sources.content: YC's view on early-stage tech DD: at Series A, the team matters more than the architecture. Architecture WILL be rewritten; teams that can do that rewrite well are rare. Look for: founders who can explain their tech tradeoffs without jargon, evidence of iteration speed (commit history, deploy frequency), and willingness to throw away code that's not working.

expected.executive_summary: Technical due diligence at Series A should weight TEAM and ENGINEERING CULTURE over architecture [2,4] — architecture will be rewritten, but the team's ability to rewrite is what matters [4]. Six areas to evaluate [1]: architecture, eng quality, team composition, security, data governance, tech debt. The hard reality from 50 DDs [3]: most Series A startups have major gaps (40% with test coverage <20%, 30% with secrets in env files, 50% with no backup procedure, 60% with bus-factor-of-one in critical areas). The bar isn't 'pristine engineering' — it's 'founders who recognize the gaps and have a credible plan'. Red flags to surface: no incident retros, deploy frequency <weekly, MTTR >24h for P1s [2]. Recommendation: ask founders to explain 3-5 of their biggest tech tradeoffs, walk through commit history + deploy frequency, demonstrate they can articulate what's bridging vs core code.

key_points.claim: Six DD areas: architecture, eng quality, team, security, data, tech debt
Engineering culture (deploy freq, MTTR, retros) reveals more than architecture diagrams
Red flags: no incident retros, deploy frequency <weekly, MTTR >24h for P1s
Common gaps at Series A: 40% have <20% test coverage; 30% have secrets in env files; 50% lack backup procedures; 60% have bus-factor-of-one
Team > architecture at Series A — architecture will be rewritten
Look for founders who can explain tradeoffs without jargon + show iteration speed

disagreements: No fundamental disagreement — sources align on team>architecture but differ in framing (process [2] vs people [4] vs gap-recognition [3]).
open_questions: What stage is the target (true Series A or later)? — bar shifts significantly
How critical is enterprise readiness (SOC 2, audit logs) at the target's stage?
Will we have engineers from our team participate in DD or rely on the founders' self-assessment?

input.topic: Conflicting evidence on remote work productivity

sources.title: Stanford WFH study — Nicholas Bloom (2023)
sources.content: Multi-year study at a 16000-employee company. Hybrid workers (2 days WFH + 3 days office) showed equivalent productivity to full-time office workers but had 33% lower attrition and 13% higher self-reported job satisfaction. Fully-remote workers showed 8% lower productivity on collaborative tasks but equivalent on focused individual work.

sources.title: Microsoft WPI productivity study (2024)
sources.content: Analysis of 60000 Microsoft employees over 2020-2024. Fully-remote work correlated with 14% MORE meetings per week, 25% MORE collaboration tools usage, but 18% LESS deep-work time (4+ hour focused blocks). Conclusion: remote shifted work from individual concentration to coordination overhead, with net productivity outcomes 'workload-dependent'.

sources.title: Goldman Sachs return-to-office study (2023)
sources.content: Internal study of investment banking division post-RTO mandate. Junior analyst output (deals handled, models built) increased 22% post-mandatory office return. Survey results: junior analysts cited mentorship access + spontaneous learning as the productivity drivers. Senior bankers (>10 years) showed no productivity difference between WFH and office.

sources.title: Meta-analysis — Journal of Applied Psychology (2024)
sources.content: Aggregates 89 studies on remote work productivity across industries. Effect sizes vary by job role: software engineers +6% remote productivity, sales -4% remote, marketing +/-0% (no significant effect), early-career employees -8% remote, senior employees +3% remote. Conclusion: 'remote work productivity' is too coarse a question — outcomes depend heavily on role + tenure.

expected.executive_summary: Evidence on remote work productivity is genuinely mixed, but the mixedness IS the signal — outcomes depend heavily on job role and career stage, not on remote-vs-office as a flat variable [4]. Software engineers see +6% productivity remote [4]; junior analysts at Goldman Sachs gained 22% productivity FROM returning to office [3]; early-career employees broadly lose 8% remote [4]. Hybrid (2 days WFH) shows the most consistent positive results: equivalent productivity + 33% lower attrition + 13% higher satisfaction [1]. Microsoft's data adds nuance: remote shifts work from focus to coordination, with the net depending on workload mix [2]. Recommendation: avoid blanket policies. Differentiate by role + tenure; hybrid is the safest default for mixed organizations.

key_points.claim: Hybrid (2 WFH + 3 office) shows equivalent productivity + 33% lower attrition + 13% higher satisfaction
Fully-remote: -8% productivity on collaborative tasks, equivalent on focused work
Remote work shifts time from deep individual work to coordination (more meetings, fewer 4+ hour focused blocks)
Junior analysts gained 22% productivity from RTO mandate; senior bankers no difference
Software engineers +6% remote productivity; sales -4%; early-career -8%; senior +3%

disagreements: Goldman Sachs data [3] shows STRONG net-positive RTO for junior analysts; Stanford [1] and meta-analysis [4] show hybrid is at least equivalent — the gap is the specific subgroup (junior + investment banking specifically) [3] vs aggregate populations [1,4].
open_questions: Which roles dominate our team composition?
What's our junior:senior ratio? (drives RTO benefit)
Is our work more 'focused individual' or 'coordination-heavy'?
